channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Landlord Law Blog Roundup for June
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

June brought several important developments for UK landlords, from property inspections to pet policies under the Renters' Reform Bill. With legislation evolving and tenant rights expanding, staying informed could help landlords navigate an increasingly complex rental landscape.

Key landlord responsibilities in June

Regular property inspections remain crucial for maintaining rental properties and identifying maintenance issues early. Whilst not legally required, thorough checks between tenancies may help prevent disputes over deposit deductions. Landlords might consider documenting the property's condition with dated photographs and detailed reports.

The month also saw discussions around rent-to-rent arrangements, where companies lease properties from landlords then sublet them. These complex agreements require careful legal consideration, particularly regarding who maintains statutory landlord responsibilities. Professional legal advice could be valuable before entering such arrangements.

Emerging tenant rights and landlord considerations

With the Renters' Reform Bill progressing, June highlighted several areas where landlords may need to adapt. The proposed right for tenants to request pets continues to generate debate. Whilst landlords wouldn't be able to unreasonably refuse, they might be able to require pet insurance or adjust tenancy terms to account for potential damage.

Another developing area involves tenant liability for property damage. A case study examined whether landlords can claim for flea infestations and damage from former tenants. The outcome often depends on the tenancy agreement's specific terms and evidence of the property's condition at check-in and check-out.

Legal experts cautioned against testing potential loopholes in the new legislation before courts have properly interpreted the rules. Landlords might find it prudent to follow established procedures rather than risk costly legal challenges during this transitional period.

Staying informed on housing law changes

With weekly news roundups covering housing policy updates, June demonstrated how rapidly the regulatory environment can change. Landlords may benefit from regular legal updates, whether through specialist blogs, trade associations or professional advisors.

As student tenancy agreements were updated this month, the importance of using current, legally compliant contracts became clear. Template agreements should be reviewed periodically to ensure they reflect the latest legislation and court rulings.

Looking ahead, landlords might consider how proposed reforms could affect their portfolios and business models. Whilst some changes remain uncertain, maintaining good property standards and clear communication with tenants could help smooth the transition to new regulations.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

June brought several important developments for UK landlords, from property inspections to pet policies under the Renters' Reform Bill. With legislation evolving and tenant rights expanding, staying informed could help landlords navigate an increasingly complex rental landscape.

Key landlord responsibilities in June

Regular property inspections remain crucial for maintaining rental properties and identifying maintenance issues early. Whilst not legally required, thorough checks between tenancies may help prevent disputes over deposit deductions. Landlords might consider documenting the property's condition with dated photographs and detailed reports.

The month also saw discussions around rent-to-rent arrangements, where companies lease properties from landlords then sublet them. These complex agreements require careful legal consideration, particularly regarding who maintains statutory landlord responsibilities. Professional legal advice could be valuable before entering such arrangements.

Emerging tenant rights and landlord considerations

With the Renters' Reform Bill progressing, June highlighted several areas where landlords may need to adapt. The proposed right for tenants to request pets continues to generate debate. Whilst landlords wouldn't be able to unreasonably refuse, they might be able to require pet insurance or adjust tenancy terms to account for potential damage.

Another developing area involves tenant liability for property damage. A case study examined whether landlords can claim for flea infestations and damage from former tenants. The outcome often depends on the tenancy agreement's specific terms and evidence of the property's condition at check-in and check-out.

Legal experts cautioned against testing potential loopholes in the new legislation before courts have properly interpreted the rules. Landlords might find it prudent to follow established procedures rather than risk costly legal challenges during this transitional period.

Staying informed on housing law changes

With weekly news roundups covering housing policy updates, June demonstrated how rapidly the regulatory environment can change. Landlords may benefit from regular legal updates, whether through specialist blogs, trade associations or professional advisors.

As student tenancy agreements were updated this month, the importance of using current, legally compliant contracts became clear. Template agreements should be reviewed periodically to ensure they reflect the latest legislation and court rulings.

Looking ahead, landlords might consider how proposed reforms could affect their portfolios and business models. Whilst some changes remain uncertain, maintaining good property standards and clear communication with tenants could help smooth the transition to new regulations.

item.title: Report urges Tories and Reform to seize ‘once-in-a-generation’ housing opportunity
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

A new policy report has highlighted what it calls a "once-in-a-generation chance" to address London's chronic housing shortage through strategic regeneration and planning reforms. The study, jointly published by centre-right think tanks Centre for Policy Studies and Onward, argues that existing powers could deliver significant housing growth if backed by stronger political commitment.

Key proposals for London's housing crisis

The 'Fixing London Housing' report outlines several major opportunities to boost housing supply in the capital:

  • Establishing new Development Corporations in Southern Tower Hamlets and along the Old Kent Road Bakerloo line extension corridor
  • Expanding powers for the Old Oak and Park Royal Development Corporation to maximise housing around transport hubs
  • Regenerating post-war housing estates to potentially deliver 500,000 additional homes through increased density
  • Releasing over 2,200 hectares of industrial land near transport links for residential development
  • Implementing five-year asset management plans for better use of public land

The proposals also suggest streamlining planning processes for brownfield sites by adjusting environmental assessment thresholds and removing certain biodiversity requirements.

Political backing for housing reforms

The report has received support from figures across the political spectrum. Conservative shadow housing secretary James Cleverly emphasised the need for "bold action" on regeneration and planning reform, noting that current decisions will affect housing availability for future generations.

Reform UK's London mayoral candidate Laila Cunningham described the housing crisis as a "political creation" that requires political solutions, arguing that London's wealth and dynamism should prevent such shortages.

With London's average house prices remaining significantly higher than national averages, the report suggests that implementing these measures could help improve affordability whilst making better use of existing urban infrastructure. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor how these proposals develop, as they could influence both housing supply and local property markets in coming years.

As the debate continues, the focus remains on whether policymakers will act on these recommendations to address one of London's most pressing challenges.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

A new policy report has highlighted what it calls a "once-in-a-generation chance" to address London's chronic housing shortage through strategic regeneration and planning reforms. The study, jointly published by centre-right think tanks Centre for Policy Studies and Onward, argues that existing powers could deliver significant housing growth if backed by stronger political commitment.

Key proposals for London's housing crisis

The 'Fixing London Housing' report outlines several major opportunities to boost housing supply in the capital:

  • Establishing new Development Corporations in Southern Tower Hamlets and along the Old Kent Road Bakerloo line extension corridor
  • Expanding powers for the Old Oak and Park Royal Development Corporation to maximise housing around transport hubs
  • Regenerating post-war housing estates to potentially deliver 500,000 additional homes through increased density
  • Releasing over 2,200 hectares of industrial land near transport links for residential development
  • Implementing five-year asset management plans for better use of public land

The proposals also suggest streamlining planning processes for brownfield sites by adjusting environmental assessment thresholds and removing certain biodiversity requirements.

Political backing for housing reforms

The report has received support from figures across the political spectrum. Conservative shadow housing secretary James Cleverly emphasised the need for "bold action" on regeneration and planning reform, noting that current decisions will affect housing availability for future generations.

Reform UK's London mayoral candidate Laila Cunningham described the housing crisis as a "political creation" that requires political solutions, arguing that London's wealth and dynamism should prevent such shortages.

With London's average house prices remaining significantly higher than national averages, the report suggests that implementing these measures could help improve affordability whilst making better use of existing urban infrastructure. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor how these proposals develop, as they could influence both housing supply and local property markets in coming years.

As the debate continues, the focus remains on whether policymakers will act on these recommendations to address one of London's most pressing challenges.

item.title: Fraudsters target homes in title hijack attempts
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New data reveals a concerning rise in property title hijacking attempts across England and Wales, with fraudsters increasingly targeting homes using sophisticated forgery techniques. According to figures obtained from HM Land Registry, criminals attempted to illegally transfer ownership or secure mortgages against 55 properties between April 2025 and March 2026 by impersonating legitimate homeowners.

How property hijacking scams work

These complex frauds typically involve criminals using forged documents to pose as genuine property owners. The most common tactics include:

  • Attempting to sell properties without the owner's knowledge
  • Securing fraudulent mortgages against the home
  • Transferring ownership through falsified paperwork

Properties considered particularly vulnerable include mortgage-free homes, buy-to-let investments and vacant properties, where owners may not immediately spot suspicious activity. One high-profile case involved a Luton vicar whose home was sold without his knowledge in 2021, with the fraud only discovered after the property had been emptied and renovation work began.

The growing threat of sophisticated fraud

Experts warn that advancing technology is making these scams harder to detect. Pete Gatenby of Novus Strategy notes that AI-generated forgeries can now bypass traditional verification systems designed to catch more basic fraud attempts. He suggests the official figures may underrepresent the true scale of the risk as fraud techniques evolve.

The current system's fragmented nature presents additional challenges, with different organisations handling separate aspects of property transactions without comprehensive data sharing. This means identity and ownership checks often occur in isolation without creating an auditable verification trail.

Homeowners concerned about title fraud might consider protective measures such as signing up for HM Land Registry's property alert service, which notifies them of certain activity relating to their home. For those with mortgage-free properties or multiple investments, regularly checking official records could help identify suspicious activity at an earlier stage.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New data reveals a concerning rise in property title hijacking attempts across England and Wales, with fraudsters increasingly targeting homes using sophisticated forgery techniques. According to figures obtained from HM Land Registry, criminals attempted to illegally transfer ownership or secure mortgages against 55 properties between April 2025 and March 2026 by impersonating legitimate homeowners.

How property hijacking scams work

These complex frauds typically involve criminals using forged documents to pose as genuine property owners. The most common tactics include:

  • Attempting to sell properties without the owner's knowledge
  • Securing fraudulent mortgages against the home
  • Transferring ownership through falsified paperwork

Properties considered particularly vulnerable include mortgage-free homes, buy-to-let investments and vacant properties, where owners may not immediately spot suspicious activity. One high-profile case involved a Luton vicar whose home was sold without his knowledge in 2021, with the fraud only discovered after the property had been emptied and renovation work began.

The growing threat of sophisticated fraud

Experts warn that advancing technology is making these scams harder to detect. Pete Gatenby of Novus Strategy notes that AI-generated forgeries can now bypass traditional verification systems designed to catch more basic fraud attempts. He suggests the official figures may underrepresent the true scale of the risk as fraud techniques evolve.

The current system's fragmented nature presents additional challenges, with different organisations handling separate aspects of property transactions without comprehensive data sharing. This means identity and ownership checks often occur in isolation without creating an auditable verification trail.

Homeowners concerned about title fraud might consider protective measures such as signing up for HM Land Registry's property alert service, which notifies them of certain activity relating to their home. For those with mortgage-free properties or multiple investments, regularly checking official records could help identify suspicious activity at an earlier stage.

item.title: After almost 40 years, estate agency unveils new identity
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A long-established Burnham-on-Sea estate agency has unveiled a fresh identity after nearly 40 years in business. Creswick Estates Ltd has rebranded as Edward Creswick & Sons, reinforcing its position as an independent family-run business whilst maintaining its High Street presence serving Burnham-on-Sea and Highbridge.

A new chapter for local property specialists

The rebrand coincides with the agency joining The Guild of Property Professionals, a national network of over 800 independent estate agents. This membership could provide enhanced marketing support and access to wider referral opportunities whilst allowing the firm to retain its local focus. The leadership team remains unchanged, with Helen Hamilton continuing to manage operations alongside Edward Creswick.

Tom Creswick maintains his role as social media and marketing consultant, working with Elisha Creswick, while William Creswick – who joined the business three years ago – remains part of the team. This continuity may reassure clients who value the agency's longstanding local expertise.

Family values at the heart of the business

Edward Creswick explained the reasoning behind putting the family name above the door: "It's about taking responsibility for our advice and delivering the service people expect from us." The move appears to reflect a commitment to personal accountability in an increasingly digital property market.

For homeowners in Somerset considering their options, independent agencies like Edward Creswick & Sons might offer an alternative to larger corporate chains. The combination of local knowledge with national network benefits through The Guild membership could prove attractive to both buyers and sellers in the area.

As the property market evolves, this rebrand demonstrates how established businesses can refresh their identity whilst maintaining the core values that have served their communities for decades.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A long-established Burnham-on-Sea estate agency has unveiled a fresh identity after nearly 40 years in business. Creswick Estates Ltd has rebranded as Edward Creswick & Sons, reinforcing its position as an independent family-run business whilst maintaining its High Street presence serving Burnham-on-Sea and Highbridge.

A new chapter for local property specialists

The rebrand coincides with the agency joining The Guild of Property Professionals, a national network of over 800 independent estate agents. This membership could provide enhanced marketing support and access to wider referral opportunities whilst allowing the firm to retain its local focus. The leadership team remains unchanged, with Helen Hamilton continuing to manage operations alongside Edward Creswick.

Tom Creswick maintains his role as social media and marketing consultant, working with Elisha Creswick, while William Creswick – who joined the business three years ago – remains part of the team. This continuity may reassure clients who value the agency's longstanding local expertise.

Family values at the heart of the business

Edward Creswick explained the reasoning behind putting the family name above the door: "It's about taking responsibility for our advice and delivering the service people expect from us." The move appears to reflect a commitment to personal accountability in an increasingly digital property market.

For homeowners in Somerset considering their options, independent agencies like Edward Creswick & Sons might offer an alternative to larger corporate chains. The combination of local knowledge with national network benefits through The Guild membership could prove attractive to both buyers and sellers in the area.

As the property market evolves, this rebrand demonstrates how established businesses can refresh their identity whilst maintaining the core values that have served their communities for decades.

item.title: Major house price index set for revamp
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

One of the UK’s most established house price indices is undergoing a significant rebrand, with the Halifax House Price Index set to be renamed as part of wider changes within Lloyds Banking Group. The move marks the end of an era for the Halifax brand, which has been a fixture in British banking since its founding in 1853.

The end of the Halifax name

Lloyds Banking Group has confirmed plans to retire the Halifax brand by 2027, with the house price index becoming the Lloyds House Price Index. This forms part of a broader strategy to consolidate Halifax Bank and Halifax Intermediaries under the Lloyds umbrella. The changes follow the group’s acquisition of Halifax in 2009, with the index remaining one of the UK’s most trusted measures of residential property values throughout this period.

Jas Singh, Lloyds Banking Group’s chief executive of consumer relationships, has sought to reassure customers that the transition will be smooth. He emphasised that account details, branch staff and digital services will remain unchanged, stating: “Our Halifax customers will keep everything they know and love today – the same fantastic app design, the same friendly faces in our branches.”

Industry reaction to the rebrand

The phasing out of the Halifax name has drawn mixed reactions from mortgage professionals. Aaron Strutt of Trinity Financial described the move as “a real shame”, noting Halifax’s historic roots as a building society and its strong reputation in the mortgage market. He suggested that while the brand might have seemed outdated, its disappearance represents a significant shift in the banking landscape.

Strutt’s comments highlight a broader trend of consolidation in the financial sector, with many well-known institutions either being acquired by larger lenders or ceasing trading in recent years. The retirement of the Halifax name after 174 years serves as a notable example of this ongoing transformation within UK banking.

For homeowners and prospective buyers, the index itself is expected to continue providing the same reliable house price data, albeit under a new name. The rebranding process is likely to be gradual, giving the market time to adjust to one of the most significant name changes in recent mortgage history.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

One of the UK’s most established house price indices is undergoing a significant rebrand, with the Halifax House Price Index set to be renamed as part of wider changes within Lloyds Banking Group. The move marks the end of an era for the Halifax brand, which has been a fixture in British banking since its founding in 1853.

The end of the Halifax name

Lloyds Banking Group has confirmed plans to retire the Halifax brand by 2027, with the house price index becoming the Lloyds House Price Index. This forms part of a broader strategy to consolidate Halifax Bank and Halifax Intermediaries under the Lloyds umbrella. The changes follow the group’s acquisition of Halifax in 2009, with the index remaining one of the UK’s most trusted measures of residential property values throughout this period.

Jas Singh, Lloyds Banking Group’s chief executive of consumer relationships, has sought to reassure customers that the transition will be smooth. He emphasised that account details, branch staff and digital services will remain unchanged, stating: “Our Halifax customers will keep everything they know and love today – the same fantastic app design, the same friendly faces in our branches.”

Industry reaction to the rebrand

The phasing out of the Halifax name has drawn mixed reactions from mortgage professionals. Aaron Strutt of Trinity Financial described the move as “a real shame”, noting Halifax’s historic roots as a building society and its strong reputation in the mortgage market. He suggested that while the brand might have seemed outdated, its disappearance represents a significant shift in the banking landscape.

Strutt’s comments highlight a broader trend of consolidation in the financial sector, with many well-known institutions either being acquired by larger lenders or ceasing trading in recent years. The retirement of the Halifax name after 174 years serves as a notable example of this ongoing transformation within UK banking.

For homeowners and prospective buyers, the index itself is expected to continue providing the same reliable house price data, albeit under a new name. The rebranding process is likely to be gradual, giving the market time to adjust to one of the most significant name changes in recent mortgage history.

item.title: Survey challenges government’s leasehold reform priorities
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research suggests leaseholders may have different priorities than those currently driving government reforms, with service charges emerging as a greater concern than ground rents for many homeowners. The Leaseholder Intelligence Report 2026, based on a survey of 2,000 leaseholders, reveals shifting perspectives on what matters most in the leasehold debate.

Service charges outpace ground rent concerns

The independent study found that 38.5% of respondents cited service charges as their most unexpectedly rising housing cost, compared to just 21% who identified ground rent as their primary concern. This challenges some assumptions underpinning current legislative proposals, with ground rent reforms having featured prominently in recent government announcements.

Perhaps surprisingly, 65% of leaseholders surveyed considered their ground rent payments affordable, while only 14% believed abolishing ground rents would make the most significant difference to their circumstances. The findings suggest that while leaseholders support reform, their immediate financial pressures may lie elsewhere in the leasehold system.

Calls for evidence-based reform

Justice for Property Rights, which commissioned the research, has urged ministers to conduct a full economic impact assessment before progressing with proposals for a 40-year sunset clause on existing ground rent income. The campaign group argues that changes affecting established property rights should be carefully evaluated for their wider implications.

Richard Merrin of Justice for Property Rights commented: "These findings show leaseholders want reforms that address their everyday concerns - particularly around service charges, transparency and building management. While supporting change, they're telling us the current debate doesn't always reflect their actual priorities."

The survey revealed that 83% of respondents were either satisfied with or neutral about their leasehold experience overall. When asked which single reform would have the greatest impact, 16.4% identified making freehold purchases easier as their top priority.

As the leasehold reform process continues, these findings may prompt policymakers to consider whether the current legislative focus aligns with the practical concerns of most leaseholders. With service charges emerging as a clear pain point, homeowners may wish to pay particular attention to how these costs are managed in their buildings.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research suggests leaseholders may have different priorities than those currently driving government reforms, with service charges emerging as a greater concern than ground rents for many homeowners. The Leaseholder Intelligence Report 2026, based on a survey of 2,000 leaseholders, reveals shifting perspectives on what matters most in the leasehold debate.

Service charges outpace ground rent concerns

The independent study found that 38.5% of respondents cited service charges as their most unexpectedly rising housing cost, compared to just 21% who identified ground rent as their primary concern. This challenges some assumptions underpinning current legislative proposals, with ground rent reforms having featured prominently in recent government announcements.

Perhaps surprisingly, 65% of leaseholders surveyed considered their ground rent payments affordable, while only 14% believed abolishing ground rents would make the most significant difference to their circumstances. The findings suggest that while leaseholders support reform, their immediate financial pressures may lie elsewhere in the leasehold system.

Calls for evidence-based reform

Justice for Property Rights, which commissioned the research, has urged ministers to conduct a full economic impact assessment before progressing with proposals for a 40-year sunset clause on existing ground rent income. The campaign group argues that changes affecting established property rights should be carefully evaluated for their wider implications.

Richard Merrin of Justice for Property Rights commented: "These findings show leaseholders want reforms that address their everyday concerns - particularly around service charges, transparency and building management. While supporting change, they're telling us the current debate doesn't always reflect their actual priorities."

The survey revealed that 83% of respondents were either satisfied with or neutral about their leasehold experience overall. When asked which single reform would have the greatest impact, 16.4% identified making freehold purchases easier as their top priority.

As the leasehold reform process continues, these findings may prompt policymakers to consider whether the current legislative focus aligns with the practical concerns of most leaseholders. With service charges emerging as a clear pain point, homeowners may wish to pay particular attention to how these costs are managed in their buildings.

item.title: Overheating moves up the PRS agenda
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research has revealed that over 441,000 privately rented properties in England currently fail to meet thermal comfort standards, with overheating becoming an increasingly pressing issue for landlords and tenants alike. The findings come as updated Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS) regulations place greater emphasis on addressing excessive heat in rental accommodation.

Growing concerns over overheating in rentals

The private rented sector faces mounting challenges regarding temperature regulation, particularly during summer months. Properties with poor insulation, inadequate ventilation or excessive glazing can become uncomfortably warm, potentially affecting tenants' health and wellbeing. The updated HHSRS guidelines now classify severe overheating as a Category 1 hazard, putting it on par with other serious housing deficiencies like damp and electrical safety issues.

Common factors contributing to overheating in rental properties include:

  • Urban heat island effects in built-up areas
  • Insufficient shading or ventilation in modern builds
  • Single-aspect flats with limited airflow
  • Properties with large south-facing windows

Landlord responsibilities and potential solutions

With overheating now formally recognised as a housing hazard, landlords may need to consider making improvements to their properties. While there's no one-size-fits-all solution, several measures could help maintain comfortable temperatures:

  • Installing reflective window film or external shading
  • Improving ventilation systems and airflow
  • Considering cool roof technology for flat-roofed properties
  • Providing portable fans as a short-term measure

Tenants experiencing significant overheating issues might wish to discuss potential remedies with their landlords. In cases where properties consistently reach unsafe temperatures, local authorities now have powers to require landlords to make necessary improvements under the updated HHSRS framework.

As climate change increases the likelihood of extreme summer temperatures, both landlords and tenants may need to pay closer attention to thermal comfort in rental properties. The updated regulations reflect growing recognition of overheating as a genuine housing concern that affects quality of life and wellbeing.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research has revealed that over 441,000 privately rented properties in England currently fail to meet thermal comfort standards, with overheating becoming an increasingly pressing issue for landlords and tenants alike. The findings come as updated Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS) regulations place greater emphasis on addressing excessive heat in rental accommodation.

Growing concerns over overheating in rentals

The private rented sector faces mounting challenges regarding temperature regulation, particularly during summer months. Properties with poor insulation, inadequate ventilation or excessive glazing can become uncomfortably warm, potentially affecting tenants' health and wellbeing. The updated HHSRS guidelines now classify severe overheating as a Category 1 hazard, putting it on par with other serious housing deficiencies like damp and electrical safety issues.

Common factors contributing to overheating in rental properties include:

  • Urban heat island effects in built-up areas
  • Insufficient shading or ventilation in modern builds
  • Single-aspect flats with limited airflow
  • Properties with large south-facing windows

Landlord responsibilities and potential solutions

With overheating now formally recognised as a housing hazard, landlords may need to consider making improvements to their properties. While there's no one-size-fits-all solution, several measures could help maintain comfortable temperatures:

  • Installing reflective window film or external shading
  • Improving ventilation systems and airflow
  • Considering cool roof technology for flat-roofed properties
  • Providing portable fans as a short-term measure

Tenants experiencing significant overheating issues might wish to discuss potential remedies with their landlords. In cases where properties consistently reach unsafe temperatures, local authorities now have powers to require landlords to make necessary improvements under the updated HHSRS framework.

As climate change increases the likelihood of extreme summer temperatures, both landlords and tenants may need to pay closer attention to thermal comfort in rental properties. The updated regulations reflect growing recognition of overheating as a genuine housing concern that affects quality of life and wellbeing.

item.title: Build-to-rent premiums double in decade
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The build-to-rent sector has seen a significant surge in rental premiums over the past decade, with figures doubling as the market continues to expand across the UK. This growth reflects both increased investment and the rising number of completed developments catering to renters seeking high-quality, purpose-built accommodation.

The rise of build-to-rent

Build-to-rent properties, designed specifically for long-term rentals, have become increasingly popular amongst tenants and investors alike. These developments often feature modern amenities, flexible tenancy terms, and professional management, making them an attractive option for those seeking a hassle-free renting experience. Over the last ten years, the sector has grown substantially, with rental premiums reflecting this upward trend.

The appeal of build-to-rent schemes lies in their ability to offer a more stable and predictable rental market compared to traditional buy-to-let properties. Investors are drawn to the potential for steady returns, whilst tenants benefit from enhanced living standards and a greater sense of security. As a result, the sector has seen a significant increase in both demand and supply.

Factors driving rental premiums

Several factors have contributed to the rise in rental premiums for build-to-rent properties. Firstly, the quality of these developments often surpasses that of older rental stock, justifying higher rental prices. Features such as communal spaces, gyms, and concierge services add value and attract tenants willing to pay a premium for convenience and comfort.

Secondly, the geographical spread of build-to-rent developments has expanded beyond major cities, reaching suburban areas and smaller towns. This broadening of the market has created new opportunities for investors whilst meeting the growing demand for rental properties in diverse locations. Additionally, the sector’s professional management model reduces the risks associated with property maintenance and tenant turnover, further enhancing its attractiveness to investors.

What does this mean for tenants and investors?

For tenants, the rising premiums may mean higher rental costs, but they also gain access to better-quality accommodation and improved living standards. Those prioritising convenience and modern amenities might find the higher costs justified, particularly in competitive rental markets.

For investors, the build-to-rent sector offers a promising avenue for long-term returns. The doubling of premiums over the past decade highlights the potential for strong yields, particularly in areas with high demand for rental properties. However, thorough research and consideration of location, market trends, and tenant demographics remain crucial for maximising investment potential.

As the build-to-rent market continues to evolve, it is likely to play an increasingly important role in the UK’s housing landscape. Both tenants and investors may wish to keep a close eye on this growing sector as it shapes the future of the rental market.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The build-to-rent sector has seen a significant surge in rental premiums over the past decade, with figures doubling as the market continues to expand across the UK. This growth reflects both increased investment and the rising number of completed developments catering to renters seeking high-quality, purpose-built accommodation.

The rise of build-to-rent

Build-to-rent properties, designed specifically for long-term rentals, have become increasingly popular amongst tenants and investors alike. These developments often feature modern amenities, flexible tenancy terms, and professional management, making them an attractive option for those seeking a hassle-free renting experience. Over the last ten years, the sector has grown substantially, with rental premiums reflecting this upward trend.

The appeal of build-to-rent schemes lies in their ability to offer a more stable and predictable rental market compared to traditional buy-to-let properties. For investors, the build-to-rent sector offers a promising avenue for long-term returns. The doubling of premiums over the past decade highlights the potential for strong yields, particularly in areas with high demand for rental properties.
